content.claim: Free school choice combined with catchment-area priority rules drives segregation more than residential distribution alone; towns with higher school-choice-driven commuting show stronger inter-school inequalities, and local policy contributes less than choice mechanics to segregation patterns.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Portuguese system reform lens
limitations: Based on analysis of Hungarian urban towns; generalizability to rural areas unclear
Does not quantify the independent contribution of choice mechanics versus track structure in producing segregation
Study does not model counterfactual scenarios without tracking
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
